# India’s strategy to repurpose subsidies for biodiversity conservation

![Photo credit: EqualStockIn](/sites/default/files/content/news_media/pexels-equalstock-20356769.jpg)

*By Dr. Ruchi Pant, Head, NRM, and Biodiversity, and Ms Vibhupriya Singh, Consultant, NRM and Biodiversity, UNDP India.*

India, home to diverse ecosystems and a rich biocultural heritage, has updated its National Biodiversity Strategies and Action Plan (NBSAP) to better align with global conservation goals. Launched at the 16th Conference of the Parties (COP) to the Convention on Biological Diversity (CBD) in Cali, this plan includes 23 National Biodiversity Targets, providing a roadmap to safeguard the country’s genetic and natural resources. It also strengthens India's commitment to the Kunming-Montreal Global Biodiversity Framework (KM-GBF).

**The impact of agricultural subsidies on biodiversity**

India's agricultural policies, including subsidies and incentives, have historically supported food security and economic stability. However, some have unintended negative impacts on biodiversity. For example:

**Understanding target 18 of the NBSAP and its significance**

Among the targets adopted at CBD COP 15, Target 18 stands out for its potential to redesign subsidies: "Identify and repurpose incentives, including subsidies, detrimental to biodiversity and scale up positive incentives for conservation and sustainable use." This is particularly significant for India’s agrarian economy, where long-standing subsidies—such as fertilizer price controls and Minimum Support Prices (MSP)—shape farming practices but may not always support biodiversity.

# Building Nature-Positive Seed Systems for Community Seed Banks in Kenya and India

Share this to :

![Community Seed Bank Opening in Kenya, 2024](https://storage.googleapis.com/cgiarorg/2024/10/Community-Seed-Bank-Opening-in-Kenya-2024.jpg)

**Harnessing the Indigenous knowledge of smallholder farmers struggling with climate change, Community Seed Banks (CSB) are at the center of in-situ agrobiodiversity conservation and development of resilient crops in Kenya and India.**

Across the diverse Kenyan and Indian landscapes, a climate-smart agricultural revolution is underway, championed by the CGIAR Initiative [Nature Positive Solutions](https://www.cgiar.org/initiative/nature-positive-solutions/) (NPS), which implements nature-positive solutions-based agrifood systems supporting local food and livelihoods. In both countries, Community Seed Banks, a vital component of NPS, are integral to preserving agrobiodiversity, serving as a cornerstone for in-situ conservation efforts critical for enhancing local economies through sustainable, nutritious, and wholesome food production and forming a foundation for sustainable agriculture through collaboration with stakeholders in Kenya, India, and beyond. These seed banks, revolutionary in their focus on safeguarding robust seeds for climate resilience, food security, and livelihoods, play a key role in developing resilient crops and protecting agrobiodiversity, thus fostering community resilience and agricultural sustainability.

**Kenya – Building Partnerships and Strengthening Communities**

Back in 2022, [Dr. Zewdie Bishaw](https://icarda.org/about-us/our-experts/zewdie-bishaw), ICARDA’s Seed Systems, International Nurseries, and Seed Health Research Team Leader, attended an [NPS](https://www.cgiar.org/initiative/nature-positive-solutions/)  inception workshop in Kisumu, Kenya, as ICARDA lead on conserving local plant varieties and enhancing seed quality while exploring commercial agri-opportunities. Partnerships with national stakeholders are crucial to the success of NPS, and ICARDA engages with local organizations like the [Kenya Agricultural and Livestock Research Organization](https://www.kalro.org/) (KALRO), [Seed Savers Network-KENYA](https://seedsaverskenya.org/), the Vihiga, Kabudi, and Nyando Community Seed Banks.

Following the inception workshop, a follow-up mission to develop a public-private-producer (PPP) partnership was carried out by [Abdoul Aziz Niane](https://icarda.org/about-us/our-experts/abdoul-aziz-niane), ICARDA’s Regional Coordinator of the Arab Peninsular Research Program, and [Seid Ahmed Kemal](https://icarda.org/about-us/our-experts/seid-ahmed-kemal), legume pathologist at ICARDA. During the mission, a PPP workshop took place with ICARDA, the Kenyan Agriculture and Livestock Research Organization (KALRO), the [Genetic Resources Research Institute](https://www.kalro.org/institutes/genetic-resources-research-institute/) (GeRRI), the Seed Saver Network (SSN), the community seed banks of Vihiga, Kabudi, and Nyando, the [Alliance of Bioversity and CIAT](https://www.cgiar.org/research/center/alliance-bioversity-ciat/). As part of the PPP mission, the ICARDA and Alliance teams visited the Community Seed Banks, discussed and agreed upon interventions such as advocacy for official government recognition of the informal seed sector, training, developing a catalog of farmer’s preferred cultivars, construction of seed processing shelters, and provision of equipment used for seed and vegetable drying, threshing, and cleaning, and long-term storage.

To strengthen Community Seed Banks and help integrate farmer’s indigenous knowledge with modern science, ICARDA supports local farmers to improve seed quality and explore new markets through training in seed science and technology, as well as linking community seed banks with local crop producers.

**India – Expanding Initiatives with Local Focus**

The ICARDA team pinpointed two promising clusters (groups of farmers) in Maharashtra: the Akole cluster in Ahmednagar and the Shahada cluster in Nandurbar district. These areas presented opportunities to enhance food security by cultivating spineless cactus in harsh conditions while supporting community initiatives to conserve native crop diversity and sustainable agricultural practices.

To address agricultural challenges tied to the NPS, the implementing agency in India, the [Bharatiya Agro Industries Foundation](https://baif.org.in/) (BAIF), arranged a field trip to the Akole cluster in September 2022 for project partners and scientists. Furthermore, a scoping report, including a baseline survey, will be submitted to CGIAR, covering aspects like drought-resistant crop varieties, conservation of native crop diversity, and technical assistance for crop suitability mapping.

**Strengthening Impact and Reach**

Community seed banks represent a revolutionary approach to the in-situ conservation of agrobiodiversity. Unlike ex-situ conservation methods, these banks place farming communities at the core of conservation and utilization efforts, emphasizing their indigenous knowledge and experience in agricultural practices. In Kenya, the focus remains on strengthening partnerships and supporting community seed banks, like the thriving projects in Vihiga, Kabudi, and Nyando, which strive to enhance capacities and infrastructure for quality seed production, processing, storage, and distribution for subsequent multiplication serving as a significant advancement. In India, the strategy involves leveraging the Amlaha platform’s strengths and expanding activities to maximize impact in target clusters.

ICARDA’s efforts under CGIAR’s Nature Positive Solutions Initiative demonstrate the power of collaboration, innovation, and persistence. By forging partnerships, advocating for supportive policies, and aiding local communities, the seeds of change we sow today hold the promise of a sustainable agricultural future in both countries.

SUMMARY:
Seed banks represent a crucial strategy in conserving plant genetic resources, vital for ensuring biodiversity, enhancing food security, and supporting

FULL CONTENT:
![](https://fsii.in/wp-content/uploads/2019/09/cropped-400logo1-federation.png)

# Importance of Seed Banks

![](https://fsii.in/wp-content/uploads/2024/04/5627-1.jpeg)

![](https://fsii.in/wp-content/uploads/2024/04/5627-1.jpeg)

Seed banks represent a crucial strategy in conserving plant genetic resources, vital for ensuring biodiversity, enhancing food security, and supporting agricultural resilience. They serve as repositories that store seeds, safeguarding the genetic diversity necessary for crop improvement and adaptation to changing environmental conditions.

## **What is a Seed Bank?**

A seed bank functions as a gene bank for seeds, storing genetic material from various plant species. This collection acts as a buffer against potential losses of biodiversity due to environmental changes, diseases, or pests. By preserving seeds, these banks maintain the genetic diversity that is crucial for the resilience of ecosystems and agricultural systems.

## **How Seeds are Stored in Banks?**

The process of storing seeds in a seed bank involves several meticulous steps to ensure their long-term viability. Initially, researchers decide which seeds to collect, often prioritizing endangered plant species. The seeds are collected when ripe, which is crucial for their viability. After collection, seeds are cleaned to remove impurities and dried in a controlled environment to reduce moisture content, which is critical for preventing mold and decay. Once dried, the seeds are sealed in airtight containers and stored at low temperatures, typically around -20°C, to slow down their metabolic rate and extend their viability. This cold storage can preserve most seeds for decades, although the exact shelf life varies depending on the seed type.

Some seeds, due to their unique characteristics, may require cryopreservation or in-vitro storage, where living plant tissues are frozen in liquid nitrogen at temperatures around -196 degrees Celsius. This method is especially useful for species that don’t produce seeds or have seeds that don’t respond well to conventional storage methods.

The world hosts over a thousand seed banks, each varying in focus and scale, from global repositories like the Svalbard Global Seed Vault to local community seed banks.

## **Need for Creating Seed Banks**

The necessity for seed banks has grown in importance due to the escalating challenges of climate change, habitat destruction, and agricultural practices that reduce genetic diversity. Modern agriculture’s reliance on monocultures and high-yield varieties has led to a significant reduction in the genetic diversity of crops, making them more susceptible to diseases, pests, and changing climatic conditions. Seed banks preserve a wide array of plant genetics, providing a resource for reintroducing species and enhancing genetic diversity within crops.

## **How Seed Banks Can Help Indian Farmers**

With reference to India, a nation with a rich agricultural heritage and diversity, seed banks hold special significance. They offer Indian farmers access to a variety of seeds, enhancing crop resilience and productivity. This diversity is especially critical for smallholder farmers, who can use diverse seed stocks to mitigate risks associated with crop failure and adapt to changing environmental conditions. Additionally, seed banks can support the conservation of indigenous plant varieties, preserving cultural heritage and traditional knowledge.

## **Seed Banks of India**

India has seen the successful implementation of community seed banks, which have empowered farmers by providing access to a range of seeds, particularly those of traditional and indigenous varieties. These initiatives have conserved many plant varieties and enhanced food security and livelihoods in rural communities.

The Indian Seed Vault is a seed bank located in Chang La in Ladakh. It was jointly built by the Defense Institute of High-Altitude Research and the National Bureau of Plant Genetic Resources in 2010, and is the second largest seed bank in the world. 

The National Gene Bank was established in the year 1996 to preserve the seeds of Plant Genetic Resources (PGR) for future generations. It has the capacity to preserve about one million germplasm in the form of seeds. Presently it is protecting 4.52 lakh accessions, of which 2.7 lakh are Indian germplasm and the rest have been imported from other countries. The world’s second-largest refurbished state-of-the-art National Gene Bank has been built at the National Bureau of Plant Genetic Resources (NBPGR), New Delhi. The revamped state-of-the-art National Gene Bank will serve the agriculture sector and the country while saving national heritage.

## **Why Farmers Should Adapt to Technical Innovations in Seed Banks**

The integration of technological innovations in seed banking offers profound benefits. Advanced techniques in seed preservation, genetic analysis, and database management can significantly enhance the efficiency and effectiveness of seed banks. For Indian farmers, embracing these innovations means better access to high-quality seeds, improved crop varieties, and enhanced ability to respond to agricultural challenges.

## **How Seeds Banks Will Benefit India as an Agriculture-Based Country**

For India, an agriculture-centric nation, seed banks are a strategic asset. They support agricultural resilience, enhance food security, and contribute to economic stability. By maintaining genetic diversity, seed banks provide a foundation for developing crop varieties that can withstand pests, diseases, and environmental stresses. This is crucial for ensuring the sustainability of agricultural production and the well-being of the vast population dependent on agriculture for livelihood.

## **Conclusion**

Seed banks hold a pivotal role in contemporary agriculture, particularly in biodiverse and agriculture-dependent countries like India. They are not just repositories of genetic material but are fundamental to the resilience of agricultural systems, the preservation of biodiversity, and the sustenance of human societies. As global challenges intensify, the strategic importance of seed banks in safeguarding the future of agriculture and food security cannot be overstated. By embracing and enhancing seed bank initiatives, nations can ensure a more resilient and sustainable agricultural future.

![](https://fsii.in/wp-content/uploads/2019/08/FSII31-300x72.png)

# India Urged To Strengthen Community Seed Banks For Climate-Resilient Food Security: CSE Report

by [Dr. Emily Greenfield](https://sigmaearth.com/author/infosigmaearth-com/ "Posts by Dr. Emily Greenfield") | May 29, 2025 | [Conservation](https://sigmaearth.com/category/conservation/), [Environmental Impact Assessment](https://sigmaearth.com/category/conservation/environmental-impact-assessment/)

The [Centre for Science and Environment (CSE)](https://www.cseindia.org/page/aboutus) in New Delhi published a significant report titled [Celebrating Community Seed Banks of India: Conversations on Climate-Resilient Seeds](https://www.cseindia.org/celebrating-community-seed-banks-of-india-conversations-on-climate-resilient-seeds-12705) on May 22, 2025, the [International Day for Biological Diversity](https://www.un.org/en/observances/biological-diversity-day). In light of growing climate hazards, the research emphasises the community seed banks’ vital role in preserving India’s food security. These community-based programs, run by farmers, self-help organizations, and nonprofits, protect traditional knowledge and Indigenous, climate-resilient seeds, safeguarding against hunger, economic instability, and climate shocks. Nonetheless, the report identifies essential obstacles that jeopardize their viability, including a lack of money, infrastructure, and legislative backing. At the inauguration ceremony, CSE and experts emphasized the importance of these seed banks in maintaining culture, [biodiversity](https://www.worldwildlife.org/pages/what-is-biodiversity), and self-sufficiency, as India is urged to strengthen community seed banks.

![India Urged to Strengthen Community Seed Banks](https://sigmaearth.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/05/c-users-admin-downloads-image-2025-05-27t123940.jpeg)

![India Urged to Strengthen Community Seed Banks](https://sigmaearth.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/05/c-users-admin-downloads-image-2025-05-27t123940.jpeg)

## **The Vital Role of Community Seed Banks**

To preserve native seed types and the related knowledge of their cultivation, community seed banks are decentralized, farmer-led projects that operate as [gene banks](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gene_bank). These banks prefer traditional seeds, which are inherently suited to endure harsh weather conditions, including [droughts](https://www.who.int/health-topics/drought#:~:text=More-,Overview,economies%2C%20energy%20and%20the%20environment.), [floods](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Flood), and [heat waves](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Heat_wave), in contrast to commercial seed systems. The actual number may be greater due to a lack of documentation. Still, ***CSE’s survey, which was carried out across 15 Indian states, indicates that these banks collectively retain around 887 climate-resilient types across 71 crops.*** According to Vijay Jardhari, the creator of the [Beej Bachao Andolan](https://www.localfutures.org/programs/global-to-local/planet-local/beej-bachao-andolan/) in Uttarakhand, “*these seeds are not just agricultural inputs; they embody culture, nutrition, and self-reliance.*” He defined bijunda, a farmer’s seed bank, as insurance against famine and climate change.

According to the report, community seed banks run reliable, generation-old processes for gathering, preserving, and distributing seeds. These systems guarantee access to various regionally adapted seeds to promote resilient farming methods. Experts like G Krishna Prasad of Sahaja Samrudha, an organic farmer collective located in Karnataka, nevertheless emphasize the necessity of official acknowledgment. Prasad called for official certification, public recognition, and inclusion in national conservation plans for farmers who are preserving indigenous cultivars to maintain community enthusiasm.

**Also Read:** [Dragon’s Blood Trees of Socotra Face Survival Threats Amid Climate Change](https://sigmaearth.com/dragons-blood-trees-of-socotra-face-survival-threats-amid-climate-change/)

## **Challenges Facing Seed Banks**

Despite their potential, community seed banks encounter several obstacles. A lack of funding and insufficient infrastructure, such as suitable storage facilities, constrains their capacity to expand operations. There is also a dearth of policy support and scant indications of official support for these grassroots initiatives. According to Vibha Varshney, CSE’s Biodiversity and Food team leader, “*access to technical support, community involvement, and government policies are essential to the sustainability of seed banks*.” She emphasized that with government assistance, the [National Bureau of Plant Genetic Resources (NBPGR)](https://www.nbpgr.ernet.in/) could play a crucial role by allowing farmers to exchange seeds from its gene bank.

The increasing popularity of hybrid and [genetically modified (GM) seeds](https://seednet.gov.in/material/GM_Seeds.htm), which are promoted as having higher yields and quicker growth, presents another difficulty. Traditional seeds are considered obsolete by younger farmers, who place more importance on immediate financial gain than long-term sustainability. “*This shift in attitude threatens the preservation of genetic diversity,*” said Shimali Chauhan, co-author of the CSE paper. [Climate change](https://www.un.org/en/climatechange/what-is-climate-change) also poses a direct threat, as variable weather patterns make seed production and storage more difficult. These difficulties highlight the necessity of coordinated efforts to keep community seed banks viable and relevant.

## **The Need for Government and Community Support**

The CSE research recommends a multifaceted strategy to develop community seed banks, and India is urged to strengthen community seed banks. The first crucial step in government action is officially acknowledging seed savers as biodiversity stewards. Farmers may be encouraged to keep conserving traditional types by offering incentives like grants or subsidies. The founder of Maharashtra’s [Vanvadi](https://vanvadi.in/), Bharat Mansata, highlighted the value of preserving genetic biodiversity as a public asset. He bemoaned that “*big buyers have easy access to our genetic wealth, but common farmers do not,*” and he called for laws that give equal access to seeds as a top priority.

Participation from the community is equally important. The appeal of commercial seeds might be resisted by including younger farmers in education and awareness initiatives. Varshney of CSE noted that a wider acceptance of indigenous seeds may result from customer demand for varied, sustainable food systems. The government may create a supportive ecosystem that balances environmental resilience and economic viability by incorporating seed banks into national biodiversity and agricultural strategies. To ensure that the wealth of Indigenous varieties is maintained for future generations, partnerships with organizations such as NBPGR could make seed exchange and documentation easier.

**Also Read:** [India Launched Draft For Climate Finance Taxonomy To Boost Green Investments And Combat Greenwashing](https://sigmaearth.com/india-launched-draft-for-climate-finance-taxonomy-to-boost-green-investments-and-combat-greenwashing/)

## **A Path to Resilient Food Systems**

In India, community seed banks are a potent instrument for creating food systems adaptable to climate change; hence, India urged to strengthen community seed banks. Diversified farming, essential for reducing climate change’s effects on agriculture, is supported by their stockpile of regional crop types. Varshney said, “*The biodiversity on our plates nourishes us,*” emphasizing the connection between nutritional security and seed diversity. These banks help farmers conserve cultural legacy, adapt to shifting environmental conditions, and lessen their reliance on outside inputs by conserving traditional seeds.

According to CSE research, community seed banks should be given top priority as the cornerstone of India’s food security plan. Policymakers, farmers, and civil society must work together to scale up these projects. Infrastructural investments in areas like digital documentation systems and climate-controlled storage could increase the banks’ efficiency. Campaigns for public awareness can also influence customer tastes towards locally produced, sustainable food, giving farmers financial incentives to keep up traditional cultivars.

It is impossible to overestimate the resilience provided by community seed banks in light of India’s increasingly unpredictable environment. They are living archives of information, culture, and survival rather than just seed stores. India can fortify these banks to guarantee food security and biodiversity for future generations by following the CSE report’s recommendations, which include official recognition, government support, and community involvement.

enriched_points.expanded_content: India's updated National Biodiversity Strategy aims to align with global conservation goals, safeguarding genetic resources while addressing the impacts of agricultural subsidies on biodiversity.
Community Seed Banks empower local farmers to conserve indigenous seeds, promoting biodiversity and resilience against climate change, crucial for enhancing food security and sustainable agriculture.

enriched_points.point_title: Ukraine's Agricultural Policy for Human Development
enriched_points.expanded_content: Ukraine's agricultural policy focuses on human development, prioritizing sustainable practices and rural community engagement to ensure food security and enhance agricultural productivity.
Regional working groups facilitate collaboration among stakeholders, sharing best practices and tailoring strategies to enhance agricultural sustainability and food security across diverse regions.
Tailored support programs address specific needs of communities, providing resources and training to improve agricultural resilience, productivity, and food security in both India and Ukraine.
